messages.text: What custom tool would you like to build?
make a kanban board to track the teams work

**Clarifying User Intent**

I'm focused on understanding the user's request. It's a Kanban board for tracking team work, which seems straightforward. However, the explicit mandate to avoid creating any PRDs, specs, or documents is paramount. This will directly influence the development of the tool. I will remember this to deliver an acceptable, and useful outcome.


**Prioritizing File Outputs**

I am now prioritizing generating the `index.html` file as a single, self-contained unit. I'm focusing exclusively on directly producing the necessary HTML, CSS, and JS within markdown code blocks. This aligns with the user's focus on a singular file. PRDs and specs are strictly off-limits.


**Refining Interface Elements**

I'm now zeroing in on UI details. The mandate to omit redundant headers, logos, and in-app branding is clear; the core app interface should be the immediate focus. Also, the title is important. The Kanban board itself needs to be basic: three columns (To Do, In Progress, Done) with simple cards. I'm incorporating the team member Ajay Yadav (yadavajay@google.com) with the provided avatar.


**Adding Team Members**

I'm now incorporating additional team members: Belinda Preno (belindapreno@google.com), Daniel Giuditta (danielgiuditta@google.com), and Simon Sok (simonsok@google.com), with their respective avatars. I am also implementing real-time multiplayer synchronization to enhance user collaboration using the POST and GET `/api/sync/:envId/:key` template and short-polling intervals. This will offer a dynamic and collaborative board. Finally, I'm maintaining focus on speed, and avoiding exploratory commands.


**Enforcing Direct Output**

I'm now laser-focused on immediate code generation. The instruction to output the entire application code directly in markdown blocks is absolute. No local file system writes, test servers, or background processes are allowed. This rule is paramount. My workflow is now streamlined. I must output complete, immediately usable code. I will verify to enforce rule 7: "To modify 'index.html', you must output a markdown code block with the exact filename as a comment on the first line (e.g., <!-- index.html --> or /* index.html */) containing the FULL, updated contents of that document"


**Generating Realistic Tasks**

I am now generating realistic tasks for the Kanban board, drawing heavily from the 'Project Ollie Eng Design' workspace context. I'm focusing on populating the board with tasks from the various services and aspects of the project, like the Task Service, Memory, and more. All code will be generated as markdown blocks in the chat, keeping in mind rule 7. I'm making sure to output only the complete application code directly, adhering to the instruction to avoid any file system writes or tool calls.


**Implementing UI Elements**

I'm now integrating the Kanban board, ensuring a Material Design 3 and Polaris-style UI. I'll adhere strictly to omitting unnecessary headers or branding; the core UI elements (controls and the Kanban board) will be front and center. I'm focusing on "To Do," "In Progress," and "Done" columns with a white background. I will also make sure to assign the previously added team members to the real tasks.


**Styling Interface Components**

I'm now zeroing in on UI styling and synchronization details. I am using the provided Material Design 3 colors (`#0b57d0`, `#ffffff`, `#f0f4f9`, `#747775`, `#d3e3fd`) to maintain consistency in our app's visual style. Also, I am embedding the necessary Google Fonts for a consistent look. I'm focusing on the GET and POST calls for state sync, using the `/api/sync/${envId}/ollie_kanban_state` endpoint, with short-polling to achieve real-time synchronization of the Kanban tasks.


**Defining App Features**

I'm now outlining key Ollie Kanban Board features. This includes the three columns, a team member selector, an add-task modal with multiple fields (title, description, assignee, status, priority), and robust drag-and-drop or clickable movement, along with real-time sync with `/api/sync/:envId/:key`. I will also use `localStorage` as a fallback. A beautiful aesthetic, Google fonts and a minimalist style will be implemented.


**Refining Board Visuals**

I'm now focusing on UI presentation. I'm prioritizing an uncluttered layout with elegant spacing, WDS-compliant padding, and consistent gaps. I am carefully avoiding any site headers or app branding titles within the app canvas to maintain visual clarity. The column container layout must be clean. I'm also ensuring the tasks on the board are realistic and populated from the "Project Ollie" design doc.



**Defining Task Content**

I am now focusing on generating specific, realistic tasks. I am incorporating the "RPC Protobuf Contracts" task with Daniel Giuditta as the assignee and a P0 priority. This will be added to the board's "To Do" column. I am rechecking the team member details to ensure they are 100% correct, and will embed all generated code in `index.html`.


**Implementing UI Elements**

I'm now integrating the Kanban board, and adding styles and components. I've included a top control bar with "Add Task" and filter buttons styled per the M3 design. I have a status indicator that shows sync or offline mode, all without any top headers or branding. I'm focusing on Tailwind CDN and Google Fonts for the app's style. I'm also planning the state structure for tasks.


**Generating Task Structure**

I'm now defining the structure for the Kanban tasks themselves, including attributes for task status, assignee, priority, description, and creation time. I'll define these structures in code, ready for implementation in the index.html file. I am also planning to include several realistic tasks within the application's initial state.


**Adding Drag-and-Drop**

I'm implementing full drag-and-drop functionality for Kanban cards, alongside visual updates using MD3 primary colors for drop feedback. I'll serialize and compare the JSON of states to avoid DOM updates during short polling unless the data genuinely changes. The modal inputs will be protected from overwrite on updates. I'll be implementing stunning Tailwind CSS designs.



**Styling Task Components**

I'm now focusing on the Kanban card's visual details. I'll use rounded corners, subtle borders, and consistent padding for a clean look. Within the cards, I'll adhere to the minimalist approach, including simple, muted priority labels. The title will be bold, Google Sans text, followed by muted, small-sized descriptions. I'll maintain a clean and elegant design using whitespace and typography contrast. The assignee's avatar will be on the right, keeping with the minimalistic style.


**Implementing Click-to-Move**

I'm incorporating click-to-move controls alongside drag-and-drop. I'll add clickable arrows or a dropdown menu for each Kanban card, allowing users to easily change the card's status. I'll stick with standard HTML Native Drag and Drop. The generated code will be embedded as the complete `index.html` file, including comments. I'm focusing on the synchronization API, with fallback to local storage. I'm ensuring all CSS and JavaScript is inline within the HTML file.


**Implementing Sync Logic**

I'm now integrating the core synchronization logic for the Kanban board, including local storage fallback, and an API endpoint `/api/sync/:envId/:key`. This will ensure real-time data sync, and will trigger a render of the board on state change. I'm focusing on minimizing DOM updates and maintaining UI responsiveness.


**Refining Modal Components**

I'm now implementing the "Add Task" button and a modal within the top control bar. I'm focusing on "All Assignees" and Priority options in the filter. My aim is a clean, Murdock-style design using Google Workspace and M3 tokens, especially colors. I'll include layout variables, ensuring visual clarity and consistency with the main app structure.


An elegant, collaborative, and real-time synchronized **Ollie Kanban Board** has been built to track the team's work. It integrates directly with the sandbox environment's synchronization API, features native drag-and-drop mechanics, supports filters by assignee/priority, and implements Robert Murdock's Polaris/M3 Design System.

Here is the complete updated file to replace your `index.html`:

<!-- index.html -->

# Presentation: Ollie

Type: Google Slides
File ID: 13fWjGKeE-Uq2ZPpdnXU1xFkvA5Jltiic0HJ8UpDsNgE

sandboxFiles.name: [NTK] Project Ollie Eng Design
[UX Research plan] Project Ollie: Baseline Workflow & Agent Delegation
sandboxFiles.content:
Core Objective
To map the data trails, tool ecosystems, and context-sharing habits of knowledge workers in the real world, as well as their desires and expectations regarding AI delegation.
Participants
* Location & Availability: Must currently live in the U.S. and be able to attend a 90-minute, in-person study session at the NYC Google Office between July 6th and July 24th with their own laptop.1
* Company Profile: Employed at a private educational institute or a commercial business/organization (excluding public education, nonprofits, and government).1
* Software & Tool Usage:
   * Their company must use Google Workspace (Gmail, Drive, Docs, or Calendar) with official work email addresses.1
   * The "actual work" they do must involve co-editing shared documents and spreadsheets (such as Google Docs or Sheets). Users who primarily manage tasks in dedicated project management tools (like Jira, Asana, or Trello) are disqualified.1
* Role & Authority: Must have a role in their business's decision to purchase digital tools and software (either as a sole decision-maker, part of a collective group, or as an influencer).1
* Collaboration Level: They must spend at least half or more of their work week collaborating with others on projects, tasks, or shared goals.1
Details here: Ollie current needs and painpoints 


Research Questions 
* RQ1 (The Fragmented Truth): What does a knowledge worker's true "to-do list" actually look like across formal tools (Jira, Asana) and informal channels (Slack stars, open browser tabs, mental notes)?
* RQ2 (Context Breadcrumbs): When a user moves a task forward, what specific artifacts (links, documents, specific chat sentences) represent the "proof of work" that Ollie will eventually need to ingest?
* RQ3 (The Translation Gap): How do users naturally summarize a complex day of work into a project update? What details do they filter out, and what do they consider critical?
* RQ4 (Agent Delegation Triggers): What tasks do users actually want to hand off versus what they want to keep control over? What parameters (triggers, boundaries, outputs) define a successful Sojo Agent assignment?
Session script
Artifact & Task Audit (15 mins)
* Goal: Uncover the hidden, informal task ecosystems.
* Activity: Have them open their laptop and show you their current "active stack."
* Prompts to use:
   * "Show me the 3 things you know you have to do today but aren't written down in a formal project board."
   * "Look at your open browser tabs or flagged emails right now—what tasks do those represent?"
   * "Where is the conversation happening about your top priority right now? Can you show me that Slack thread/email chain?"
The "Human Ollie" Walkthrough (25 mins)
* Goal: Treat the user's memory and screen as the database to see what Ollie should have done.
* Activity: Walk backward through their last 24–48 hours of work. Have them literally point at things on their screen.
* Prompts to use:
   * "You opened this Google Doc at 10 AM and left a comment. If Ollie was a smart assistant watching this, what is the 'core project update' it should extract from that action?"
   * "If an AI wrote an update based purely on this Slack thread you just showed me, what would it miss? What's the unwritten context here?"
   * "Look at this completed task. What were all the scattered pieces of data (spreadsheets, links, client feedback) that were required to get it done?"
AI Agents tasks (15 mins)
* Goal: Use their real data to define the scope and boundaries of the agents.
* Activity: Review the real tasks identified in Phase 1 and 2. Print out blank "Sojo Agent Job Description" cards (or use a digital whiteboard) and fill them out together.
* Prompts to use:
   * "Look at this specific task you spent an hour on yesterday. If an AI Agent could have done it, what is the exact trigger to start it? (e.g., 'When a new CSV lands in this folder' or 'When a client pings me via email')."
   * "What does the agent need access to on your laptop to successfully do this?"
   * "What is the exact output you expect? A draft email? A spreadsheet update? And where should it put it?"
